explanation: The property requires that before the Local Node generates and sends an HELLO message, it must have queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set (in that order or at least all three before message send). If an HELLO message ('hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') is sent without this sequence, it is an error (which means, the formula must prevent that situation from occurring at any time, i.e., globally). We encode this by stating: Globally, whenever 'hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' occurs, it is implied that at some time previously ('F' in the past, but since standard LTL is future-looking, we use that globally, at any time, if a Hello is sent, then each query occurs before or at that moment). This is typically encoded as: Globally (if a Hello is sent, then previously, all the queries have occurred). We can encode sufficient correctness as: It is never the case that a Hello message is sent and one of the queries (query Link Set, Neighbor Set, or MPR Set) did not occur before. To express this in standard LTL: For every point in time, if a Hello is sent, then in the past there was a point where each query has manifested and not been reset. With no past operators, we express this as: After the system starts, the queries 'local_node_queried_link_set', 'local_node_queried_neighbor_set' and 'local_node_queried_mpr_set' must all occur before any 'hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi'. In standard LTL, this is expressed as: It is never the case that a Hello message is sent before each of those queries occurs. We can express this as: (!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_link_set & (!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & (!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_set)))) Or, globally: For all times, if a Hello is sent, then previously, all the queries have occurred. More intuitively, requiring that between Hello messages, all three queries must occur. For our purposes, an LTL formula expressing 'A Hello message is only sent after Link, Neighbor, and MPR sets have all been queried since the previous Hello message' is: G(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (P1 & P2 & P3)) where each Pn is 'the corresponding query occurred since last Hello or system start'. To encode this strictly using future-only LTL, we use an invariant expressing that it never happens that a Hello is sent before requisite queries: G((!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_link_set & (!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & (!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U local_node_queried_mpr_set))))

explanation_dict.natural_language: Local Node queried Link Set
Local Node queried Neighbor Set
Local Node queried MPR Set
HELLO message sent from Local Node to DM_i
Before generating/sending a HELLO message, these queries must occur
Globally, an error occurs if a HELLO message is exchanged without these queries

explanation: The property requires that for every DM_i, before an HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must have queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components. An error occurs if an HELLO message is exchanged without this chain. This can be encoded by saying: Globally, whenever an HELLO message is sent from DM_i to Local Node, it must be the case that, in the past, the Local Node has queried Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set. Because LTL is future-oriented (no direct past), we can rephrase it as: It is always the case that if an HELLO message is sent, then it must have been preceded (at some time before, possibly immediately before) by a query to these three sets. Alternatively, to enforce that queries must occur before HELLO message is sent, we can require that HELLO message cannot be sent until (U) all three queries have occurred. Assuming that querying the sets causes a propositional variable to become true, and that these should all happen before the HELLO message, the formula becomes: Globally, HELLO message is not sent until all three queries have been performed. Alternatively, for each HELLO message, it must be the case that previously (just before HELLO), all three queries occurred. As LTL lacks the past fragment, the most common way is to prevent the HELLO message until all queries are met. The result is: G((!hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)) or, to make it clear for every occurrence: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set) & (!hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)))). However, the standard idiom is to write: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)) assuming they all occur at the same time or just before. If they occur at different instants (as a sequence), it is stricter to say that HELLO message is not sent until all three have occurred: G((!hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)). This is the closest standard LTL for 'not until'.

explanation_dict.natural_language: before generating the message, Local Node must query the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components
An HELLO message is sent from DM_i to Local Node
HELLO message is not sent until all three queries have been performed
This property holds globally (for every DM_i)

explanation:
The property requires that every HELLO message that is sent from DM_i to the Local Node must be preceded (in the correct order) by queries from the Local Node to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set. Moreover, an error occurs otherwise. We express the correct ordering as: whenever a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, in the past the queries to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set must have all occurred (in order). In LTL, this is typically expressed via implication: globally, if a HELLO message is sent, then previously the three queries in the required order have happened.

Since standard LTL does not have a direct 'past' operator, we can encode 'must happen before' by requiring that, globally, whenever hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, it is always preceded by the required queries (i.e., it is not possible for hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node to occur without the queries having occurred first). We can capture this with a global constraint that whenever hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, the queries occur in the immediately preceding three steps (using X, i.e., 'next'), or, more generally, that these queries must all occur somewhere before the message. However, in LTL with only future-time operators, the most straightforward equivalent is: it is never the case that hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs before the three queries. Or, globally, if hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then previously the queries must have happened (in order). 

A workaround in standard LTL is to use a monitor variable that tracks whether all three queries have been performed; but with only the standard operators, we can express: globally, it is not the case that hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs before local_node_queried_link_set, local_node_queried_neighbor_set, and local_node_queried_mpr_set have all occurred (in order).

Thus, the final formula is:
G((!hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) | (F(local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set))))

But in terms of implication, this can be rewritten as:
G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set))))

But this would mean the queries may still happen after the message, which is not sufficient. Thus, we instead require that it must never happen that hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node holds and it is not the case that the queries have occurred before (in the correct order). However, as standard LTL cannot express past explicitly, the best-fitting formula for future LTL is to require that the only way hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is ever true is after the queries. That is, in every state where hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is true, previously the queries have occurred (since we cannot express past operators, this cannot be precisely encoded). 

Therefore, the weakest future LTL version is:
G((local_node_queried_link_set & X(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X(local_node_queried_mpr_set))) -> F hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)

But to force the chain before the hello message, the best LTL expressible form is to prohibit a hello message unless the chain has just occurred. Thus:
G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> Y(local_node_queried_mpr_set & Y(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & Y local_node_queried_link_set)))

But since we do not have past ('Y') operators, the strongest future-only expression is:
Globally, it is never the case that hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs before the chain local_node_queried_link_set; local_node_queried_neighbor_set; local_node_queried_mpr_set.

Thus, for standard LTL (using only future operators), we encode:
Globally, whenever hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, the previous three steps were the queries (in order):
G( X(X(X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node )) -> (local_node_queried_link_set & X(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X(local_node_queried_mpr_set))))

Alternatively, wherever hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, the three queries must have just occurred in the three previous steps. But with standard LTL, an acceptable approximation is:
Globally, hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node never occurs unless the three queries occurred before.

Thus, the most accurate in standard LTL is:
G( (local_node_queried_link_set & X(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X(local_node_queried_mpr_set & X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))) -> true )

or, as an allowed sequence:
Globally, it is always the case that hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is only sent after the three queries, i.e., the three queries must occur in strict order before a hello message is sent.

That is: G( (local_node_queried_link_set & X(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X(local_node_queried_mpr_set & X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))))

This expresses that the only allowed occurrence of hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node is after the three queries in order.

Therefore, final answer:
G( (local_node_queried_link_set & X(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X(local_node_queried_mpr_set & X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))))

explanation_dict.natural_language: Local Node queried Link Set, Neighbor Set, then MPR Set
then a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node
Globally, the only allowed sequence for a hello message is after this chain of queries

explanation:
The specification requires that before the Local Node generates (i.e., sends) a HELLO message to DM_i, it must first query the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components, in that order. If a HELLO message is sent without this sequence, it is an error. We encode the following temporal property: It is never the case that a HELLO message is sent without the prior occurrence (in any previous moment since the last message) of queries to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set. We interpret this as: Globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, in the past, each of these three queries must have occurred in the correct order with no other occurrence of sending the message in-between. Since LTL cannot express 'past' directly, but can express safety properties over traces, we can specify it as: For any point where a HELLO message is sent, it cannot be the case that any of the necessary queries are missing since the last send. In standard LTL, as commonly accepted, we usually write: it is never the case that a HELLO is sent and one of the queries has not (yet) happened since the previous HELLO send. To formalize in LTL, we enforce that a HELLO message can only be sent when, since the previous HELLO send, queries to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set have all occurred. This property is known as a response chain property. We use three auxiliary flags, but in pure LTL, we can only specify directly as: 'it is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then previously Link Set, Neighbor Set and MPR Set queries have occurred since the last HELLO message.'

But since LTL is future-directed, we can, equivalently, specify: Between any two sends of a HELLO message, the three queries must occur. This can be specified as: It is always the case that after a HELLO message is sent, until the next HELLO message is sent, the queries to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set must all happen. We encode this by requiring, globally, that after a HELLO is sent, before it is sent again, each query eventually happens.

This leads to the formula:
G(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (F local_node_queried_link_set & F local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set) U hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)

Alternatively, to be more strict and allow reasoning from every moment, we specify for all time, between hello_msg_sent events, the three queries must occur. (Another, even stricter, way is: It is never the case that a HELLO message is sent and, since the previous HELLO message was sent, one of the queries did not happen. Since LTL does not have history, our formula expresses the intended future requirement.)

output_LTL: G(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> ((F local_node_queried_link_set & F local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set) U hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi))

explanation:
The requirement is that before generating (sending) a HELLO message, Local Node must first query the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set. If a HELLO message is sent without this order of queries, it's considered an error. We want to say: 'It is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then in the (finite) sequence leading up to this HELLO message, the Local Node must have (most recently) queried Link Set, then Neighbor Set, then MPR Set, without interleaving a HELLO message in between.' To capture this in LTL, a standard approach is to express that, globally, every time a HELLO message is sent, it was preceded (in the previous steps) by (1) a query to MPR Set, (2) before that, a query to Neighbor Set, (3) before that, a query to Link Set, and between those queries and the HELLO message no other HELLO message is sent. Since LTL does not have an explicit 'previous' operator, we typically encode the ordering by stating: Whenever a HELLO message is sent, in the past there must have been the queries in the required order and no HELLO message sent in between. Equivalent in future temporal logic: It is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then (in the past) there was a sequence of Link Set query, then Neighbor Set query, then MPR Set query, and only then the HELLO message is sent. In LTL (over infinite traces), we can best approximate this by the following pattern: For every HELLO message sent, in all paths, it must be preceded by the triple of queries, meaning that it is not possible to send a HELLO without the three queries beforehand. We encode this using '! (a && (!b U c))'-like patterns or the 'leads to' pattern. The simplest LTL formula, given our operators, is: 'Globally, if a HELLO message is sent, then previously (since the last HELLO message, or globally if first) the three queries occurred in order.' We can write, for every time a HELLO message is sent, it must be the case that, since the last HELLO message, (local_node_queried_link_set happened before local_node_queried_neighbor_set, which happened before local_node_queried_mpr_set) and then the HELLO message is sent. This is customarily expressed as: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (P local_node_queried_link_set & P local_node_queried_neighbor_set & P local_node_queried_mpr_set )) but in basic LTL (with no Past operators), the best we do is: G( (last_action_is_local_node_queried_link_set -> F( local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F( local_node_queried_mpr_set & F hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node ) ) ) ). But since we only have future operators, a sufficient way is: It is globally not possible to send a HELLO message unless the three queries have previously happened since the last HELLO (or since the start). This can be approximated in LTL by stating: It is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then before it is sent, there has been (most recently) a query to MPR Set, which was preceded by a query to Neighbor Set, which was preceded by a query to Link Set. This is typically captured with auxiliary automata, but in pure LTL, we can approximate with: G( hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F(local_node_queried_mpr_set & X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)))) ) but this does not fully capture the 'in order, directly preceding'. Instead, in practical LTL, such a property is best encoded as: 'It is always the case that if local_node_queried_link_set occurs, then eventually local_node_queried_neighbor_set occurs, then eventually local_node_queried_mpr_set occurs, then eventually hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, and between these events, no other hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs.' For core LTL, a good enough encoding is:
G( ( (local_node_queried_link_set) -> F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F(local_node_queried_mpr_set & F hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))) )
But this could allow irrelevant queries in between. The core safety property is best stated as: It is never the case that a HELLO message is sent without having the three queries in order since the last HELLO. For simplicity, we encode: 'It is always the case that if hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, then in the preceding steps (i.e., before this), there was (possibly interleaved with other things) local_node_queried_link_set, then after that local_node_queried_neighbor_set, then after that local_node_queried_mpr_set, all before the HELLO message.'
So, with only future LTL, a reasonable simple formula is:
G ( hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> ( P1 & P2 & P3 ) )
where P1=(local_node_queried_link_set has happened), P2=(local_node_queried_neighbor_set has happened after P1), P3=(local_node_queried_mpr_set has happened after P2), all before hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node. Since no past, we can encode that 'HELLO never occurs before the three queries in order' as:
G( !( hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node & ( !(local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F(local_node_queried_mpr_set))) ) ) )
Or, more readable:
G( (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set))) )
But this requires the queries after the HELLO, so it's not strictly correct. Pure LTL is limited, so the most standard encoding (for a safety property) is:
It is always NOT the case that a HELLO message is sent BEFORE the three queries have occurred in the correct order.
We can specify that at all times, until all three queries occur, no HELLO message can be sent:
((!(local_node_queried_link_set)) U (local_node_queried_link_set & ( (!(local_node_queried_neighbor_set)) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & ( (!(local_node_queried_mpr_set)) U (local_node_queried_mpr_set & X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)) )) ))
But this is rather complex. For this example, the most widely used simple LTL formulation is:
G( (local_node_queried_link_set -> F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F(local_node_queried_mpr_set & F hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node))) )
But the closest, standard readable LTL formula that enforces the sequence before the HELLO is:
G( (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set)))) )
But ideally, we want to say that HELLO cannot be sent until local_node_queried_link_set happens, then local_node_queried_neighbor_set, then local_node_queried_mpr_set, then HELLO.
So, collect the order as a 'chain' of untils:
Let A=local_node_queried_link_set
Let B=local_node_queried_neighbor_set
Let C=local_node_queried_mpr_set
Let H=hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node
We can write:
¬H U (A & (¬H U (B & (¬H U (C & (¬H U H))))))
But in LTL safety, the best canonical form is:
G( (¬local_node_queried_link_set & ¬local_node_queried_neighbor_set & ¬local_node_queried_mpr_set) U (local_node_queried_link_set & (¬local_node_queried_neighbor_set & ¬local_node_queried_mpr_set) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & (¬local_node_queried_mpr_set U (local_node_queried_mpr_set U hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)))) )
But for readable clarity, use:
G( (¬hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_link_set & ((¬hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & ((¬hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_mpr_set & X hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)) ))) )
But for **simplicity** (and widespread tool support), the most typical is:
G( hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set)))
Summary: Safety properties of this type are challenging in pure future LTL, but the formula above is commonly used for such precondition chains.

then must query Neighbor Set

output_LTL: G( hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F local_node_queried_link_set & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F local_node_queried_mpr_set)) )

explanation: The property describes that, before a HELLO message is sent by the Local Node to DM_i, the Local Node must have queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components. An error occurs if this querying sequence does not happen prior to the HELLO message. This must hold globally for every DM_i. In LTL, this is expressed by stating that whenever a HELLO message is sent ('hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi'), it must have been preceded by the three queries ('local_node_queried_link_set', 'local_node_queried_neighbor_set', 'local_node_queried_mpr_set'). In LTL, we use the 'historically' operator (which isn't standard) or, equivalently, we say that it is never the case that a HELLO message is sent unless all three queries have already occurred before. This can be encoded with the 'until' operator and negations: it is never the case that a HELLO message is sent unless all queries have already been performed since the last HELLO message. A common LTL idiom for such a 'must-precede' condition is: globally, 'if a HELLO message is sent, then in the past (since the last HELLO message or since the beginning) all three queries have occurred.' For LTL without past, we can express that it is never the case that a HELLO is sent before all the queries using a safety property. One option in LTL without past is: before any 'hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi', we first see all three queries (in any order, but all have happened). To simplify, we can say 'It is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then before that sending, each required query has occurred.' In LTL, using 'G' and implication, and using 'F' (finally), we can state as: 'It is always the case that if a HELLO is sent, then previously the three queries happened since the last HELLO message.' In practice, with only future operators (G, F, X, U, etc), a commonly used form is: 'Globally, it is never the case that a HELLO is sent before the three queries.' This can be expressed by: For all futures, (if not all three queries have occurred, then HELLO message is not sent). The formula becomes: G((!local_node_queried_link_set | !local_node_queried_neighbor_set | !local_node_queried_mpr_set) -> !hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) This means: as long as not all queries have happened, no HELLO message is sent.

explanation_dict.natural_language: Local Node queried Link Set
Local Node queried Neighbor Set

HELLO message sent from Local Node to DM_i
If the three queries have not happened, a HELLO message must not be sent

explanation: The requirement states that before generating (sending) an HELLO message from DM_i to the Local Node, the node must query the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components in order. An error occurs if a HELLO message is exchanged without this sequence. This property should hold globally (for every point in time, for every DM_i). Thus, it means that whenever a HELLO message is sent, the queries to Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set must have happened previously, in that order, with no HELLO message occurring before the sequence. This is best captured by stating: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, in the past, there was a sequence where the Local Node queried the Link Set, then queried the Neighbor Set, then queried the MPR Set, and only then did the HELLO message occur. In standard LTL (which is future only), we can instead characterize it as: it is not possible for a HELLO message to be sent before the queries. We encode this as: G( (hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) -> (local_node_queried_link_set && F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set && F(local_node_queried_mpr_set && X(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)))) ). But since all three queries must happen before the HELLO, and in order, a stronger and simpler LTL formula aligned with patterns is: globally, if a HELLO message is sent, then prior to that (in the same state or before), the queries happened in order. In LTL, the requirement that 'a' occurs only after 'b', 'c', 'd' (in order) translates to: G( !(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) U (local_node_queried_link_set && X( (!(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set && X( (!(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)) U (local_node_queried_mpr_set && X(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node)) ) ) ) ) ). This means: HELLO cannot occur until LS queried, then HELLO cannot occur until NS queried (after LS), then HELLO cannot occur until MPR queried (after NS), and finally HELLO can occur. This encodes the required chain before HELLO. (Note: This translation assumes possible consecutive states for queries and message send events.)

explanation_dict.natural_language: Local Node queried Link Set

explanation: The property requires that in every behavior (globally), before a HELLO message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must have queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components. If a HELLO message is sent without these queries, an error occurs. This can be formalized by stating: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, it must be preceded (possibly immediately or after some time) by these three queries. In LTL, we can express this as: it is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then, at some point in the past (since LTL is future-looking, we rephrase: 'It is not the case that a HELLO message is sent before the three queries have occurred'), i.e., the HELLO message should not occur before all three queries. This can be expressed as: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, in the past there was a querying of Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set. Since basic LTL doesn't have past operators, we express this as: 'It is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then, previously, all three queries have occurred and not the case that a HELLO message can occur before any of the three queries.' In standard future LTL over infinite traces, this is typically approximated by: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, the three queries have occurred previously (which is difficult to express directly). Alternatively, to ensure ordering, we can require that before HELLO message is sent, the three queries are performed each time, i.e., HELLO message cannot occur before these. We formalize this as: 'It is always the case that if not all three queries have occurred, then a HELLO message cannot occur.' This is done by defining that until the three queries all occur, a HELLO message cannot occur. That is, until all three (Link Set, Neighbor Set, MPR Set) are queried, HELLO message is not sent. So, the main formula is: ((!(local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)) U (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)) -> !(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) until (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set). But we can simplify: "It is globally the case that a HELLO message is never sent until all three queries have happened"; that is: G( !hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node U (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set) )

Expressing 'must query ... before ...' in LTL (without past operators) as 'HELLO message not sent until all queries have occurred'.
This property holds globally for every DM_i.

explanation: The property requires that, for every DM_i, the HELLO message ('hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node') should only be sent after the Local Node has queried the Link Set ('local_node_queried_link_set'), Neighbor Set ('local_node_queried_neighbor_set'), and MPR Set ('local_node_queried_mpr_set'). If a HELLO message is sent without this chain of queries happening before, it is an error. In LTL, this is usually modeled as: globally, whenever a HELLO message is sent, it must have been preceded by all three queries in some order (we'll require all to happen before). To express 'must be queried before', we use absence (¬) of HELLO message until all queries have occurred, or after all queries have happened once, the message can happen any number of times. The direct way is: it is always the case (G) that if a HELLO message is sent, then previously all three queries happened. This can be modeled as: G(hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (P local_node_queried_link_set & P local_node_queried_neighbor_set & P local_node_queried_mpr_set)), but as LTL does not have 'previous (P)' operator, but can be expressed in safety style as: as long as not all of the queries have happened, a HELLO message must not be sent. That is: G((!(local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)) -> !hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node). Alternatively, after all three queries have occurred, the HELLO message is allowed. This leads to the equivalent LTL formula: G((hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) -> (F(local_node_queried_link_set) & F(local_node_queried_neighbor_set) & F(local_node_queried_mpr_set))). But we really want that each individual HELLO message event is preceded by these queries, so the safety LTL property is: It is never the case that a HELLO message is sent and at least one query hasn't happened yet. That is: G(¬hello_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node | (local_node_queried_link_set & local_node_queried_neighbor_set & local_node_queried_mpr_set)).

explanation: The property requires that before the Local Node sends an HELLO message to DM_i, it must have previously queried the Link Set, Neighbor Set, and MPR Set components in that order. If a HELLO message is sent without this chain, it is an error and should not happen. This should hold globally for every DM_i. In LTL, this is typically specified by saying: it is always the case (G) that if a HELLO message is sent (hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi), then in the past (expressed here by ensuring that before any HELLO message, querying actions have occurred without being interrupted by another HELLO message), the sequence of queries happened. Since standard LTL does not have 'past' operators, we express this as: after the sequence of queries, only then a HELLO message can be sent. So, at any moment, a HELLO message can only be sent if the three queries have just happened, and after any HELLO message, the sequence must be re-done before the next HELLO message. This is enforced as: It is always the case that if a HELLO message is sent, then in the strict past, the three queries occurred without another HELLO message in between, or (equivalently, in safety form): It is always the case that if the three queries have not happened in order since the last HELLO message, a HELLO message must not be sent. In standard safety style, this is best expressed as: it is never the case that a HELLO message is sent and, since the last HELLO message, there does not exist an execution where querying Link Set happened, then querying Neighbor Set, then querying MPR Set (in order) before the HELLO message. This can be expressed in positive LTL as: G (hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (F (local_node_queried_link_set & F (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & F (local_node_queried_mpr_set & X (!hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi U hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)))))). But to better capture the sequence, we want: after a HELLO message, the three queries must all occur (in order) before the next HELLO message. This can be expressed as: G(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> X ((!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)) U (local_node_queried_link_set & X (!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X (!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_set & X (!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)))))))) But typically, for expected behavior, the property can be given as: whenever a HELLO message is sent, before that (since the last HELLO message), the three query events must have occurred in the right order. In LTL without past, commonly, we encode as: Between any two HELLO messages, the sequence of three queries must occur. That is, between every two successive HELLO messages, querying Link Set, then Neighbor Set, then MPR Set must happen in that order. This can be encoded as: G(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> X (((!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_link_set & X((!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_neighbor_set & X((!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_set & X((!(hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U hello_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi)))))))))))) This ensures that after any HELLO message, the sequence of queries must occur again before the next one. (If you want to ensure the queries happen *just before* sending the HELLO, a common relaxed version is: Its always the case that if a HELLO is sent, then there exists in the past (but since LTL does not have past) a sequence of three queries before the previous HELLO.)
